blocks:
Ayahs

لَمْ يَكُنِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ مُنفَكِّينَ حَتَّىٰ تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَةُ (١)
The faithless from among the People of the Book and the polytheists were not set apart [from the community of the faithful] until the proof had come to them: (1)
رَسُولٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ يَتْلُوا۟ صُحُفًا مُّطَهَّرَةً (٢)
an apostle from Allah reciting impeccable scriptures, (2)
فِيهَا كُتُبٌ قَيِّمَةٌ (٣)
wherein are upright writings. (3)
وَمَا تَفَرَّقَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَةُ (٤)
Those who were given the Book did not divide except after the proof had come to them. (4)
وَمَآ أُمِرُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ حُنَفَآءَ وَيُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ دِينُ ٱلْقَيِّمَةِ (٥)
Though they were not commanded except to worship Allah, dedicating their faith to Him as men of pure faith, and to maintain the prayer and pay the zakat. That is the upright religion. (5)

Content and Virtue of Surah Al-Bayyinah

The prevailing view is that this Surah was revealed in Medina, and its content bears witness to this fact, as it repeatedly discusses the People of the Book (Ahl al-Kitab), and we know that the Muslims' primary interactions with the People of the Book occurred in Medina.

Furthermore, it mentions both Prayer (Salat) and Alms-giving (Zakat); while it is true that Zakat was legislated in Mecca, its formal establishment and expansion took place in Medina.

In any case, this Surah refers to the universal mission of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and its being accompanied by clear proofs and signs—a mission they had previously been awaiting, yet when it came to them, a group turned their backs on it because their material interests were at stake.

Additionally, it contains the truth that the fundamental principles of the prophets' call, such as faith, monotheism, prayer, and fasting, are constant and eternal principles that have existed in all heavenly religions.

In another section of this Surah, it defines the various stances of the People of the Book and the polytheists toward Islam: those who believed and performed righteous deeds are the best of creatures, while those who chose the path of disbelief, polytheism, and sin are considered the worst of creatures.

This Surah has several names chosen in accordance with its words, but the most famous are Surah "Al-Bayyinah," "Lam Yakun," and "Al-Qayyimah."

In a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it is narrated:

"If people knew what blessings this Surah contains, they would leave their families and wealth to devote themselves to learning it!" A man from the tribe of Khuza'ah asked: "O Messenger of Allah! What is the reward for reciting it?"

He replied: "No hypocrite recites it, nor those in whose hearts is doubt. By Allah, the near-stationed angels have been reciting it since the day the heavens and the earth were created, and they do not slacken for a moment in its recitation. Whoever recites it at night, Allah appoints angels to protect his religion and his worldly life, and to seek forgiveness and mercy for him. And if he recites it during the day, he is given a reward equivalent to what the day illuminates and the night darkens." [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 521.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

لَمْ يَكُنِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ وَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ مُنْفَكِّينَ حَتَّى تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ (1) رَسُولٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ يَتْلُوا صُحُفاً مُطَهَّرَةً (2) فِيها كُتُبٌ قَيِّمَةٌ (3) وَ ما تَفَرَّقَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ إِلاَّ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ (4)

وَ ما أُمِرُوا إِلاَّ لِيَعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ حُنَفاءَ وَ يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ يُؤْتُوا الزَّكاةَ وَ ذلِكَ دِينُ الْقَيِّمَةِ (5)

Commentary

This is the Eternal Religion

At the beginning of the Surah, addressing the state of the People of the Book (Jews and Christians) and the Arab polytheists before the advent of Islam, it says: "They claimed: 'We will not abandon our creed until a clear proof and a manifest prophet comes to us'" (لَمْ يَكُنِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ وَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ مُنْفَكِّينَ حَتَّى تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ).

"A messenger from Allah, reciting pure and sanctified pages to us" (رَسُولٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ يَتْلُوا صُحُفاً مُطَهَّرَةً).

"Pages in which are correct, balanced, firm, and precious writings" (فِيها كُتُبٌ قَيِّمَةٌ).

Yes, they held such a claim before the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, but after his appearance and the revelation of His heavenly Book, the scene changed, and they differed regarding the religion of God: "And the People of the Book did not differ except after the clear proof and the truthful, manifest Prophet came to them" (وَ ما تَفَرَّقَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ).

Thus, the above verses recount the claim of the People of the Book and the polytheists who initially insisted that if a prophet were to come to call them with clear proofs, they would accept.

However, after his arrival, they reneged on this promise and rose in opposition and strife against him, except for a group who took the path of faith.

Therefore, the above verse is similar to what appears in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 89:

وَ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ كِتابٌ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَهُمْ وَ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ ما عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ فَلَعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ‌:

"And when there came to them a Book from Allah confirming that which was with them—although before they used to pray for victory against those who disbelieved—when there came to them that which they recognized, they disbelieved in it; so the curse of Allah be upon the disbelievers."

We know that the People of the Book were awaiting such an advent, and naturally, the Arab polytheists, who considered the People of the Book more learned and aware than themselves, echoed them in this agenda. But after their desires were realized, they changed their course and joined the ranks of the opponents.

A group of commentators holds another view in interpreting these verses, which is:

The meaning is that they would not actually—not just by claim—abandon their creed and be "separated" or detached until a clear proof came to them.

However, the implication of this statement would be that they believed after the arrival of such a clear proof, whereas the subsequent verses show that this was not the case—unless it is said that the meaning refers to the faith of a group of them, even if they were a minority (a "particular affirmative" proposition). Nevertheless, this interpretation seems unlikely, and perhaps for this reason, Fakhr al-Razi in his commentary considers the first verse among the most complex verses of the Quran as it seemingly contradicts the following verses, and then mentions ways to resolve the problem, the best of which is what we mentioned above.

There is also a third interpretation here: the meaning is that Allah does not leave the polytheists and the People of the Book to themselves until He has completed the proof (atmam al-hujjah) upon them, sent a clear proof, and shown them the way; hence He sent the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله for their guidance.

In reality, this verse refers to the "Rule of Grace" (Qa'idah al-Lutf) discussed in theology (Kalam), stating that Allah will send clear proofs to every people and nation to complete the proof [1]. In any case, the meaning of "Bayyinah" here is "clear proof," the manifestation of which, according to the second verse, is the person of the "Messenger of Allah" while he had the Holy Quran upon his tongue.

[1]: It should be noted that "munfakkin," the plural of "munfakk," can be an active participle (ism fa'il) or a passive participle (ism maf'ul). According to the first and second interpretations, it has the meaning of an active participle, and according to the third interpretation, it has the meaning of a passive participle (reflect on this).

"Suhuf" is the plural of "sahifah," meaning pages upon which something is written, and what is meant here is the content of the pages, for we know that the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله never read anything from physical pages.

The meaning of "mutahharah" (purified) is its being pure from any polytheism, falsehood, lies, and vanity, and purified from the interference of the devils among jinn and men.

As stated in verse 42 of Surah Fussilat: لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَ لا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ‌: "Falsehood cannot come to it from before it or from behind it."

The phrase "فِيها كُتُبٌ قَيِّمَةٌ" indicates that in these heavenly pages, matters are written that are free from any deviation, distortion, or crookedness.

Therefore, "kutub" means "writings" (maktubat) or the laws and regulations determined by Allah, as "kitabah" (writing) also comes with the meaning of determining a decree, as He says: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيامُ كَما كُتِبَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ‌ "Fasting is prescribed for you as it was prescribed for those before you" (Baqarah: 183).

Accordingly, "qayyimah" means straight and upright, or firm and stable, or valuable and precious, or all these concepts combined.

The possibility has also been raised that since the Quran contains the entire content of previous books with many additions, it is said that within it are the "qayyimah" (upright) books of the past.

It is noteworthy that in the first verse, the "People of the Book" are mentioned before the "polytheists," and in the fourth verse, only the People of the Book are mentioned while the polytheists are not, even though the verse applies to both.

These expressions are apparently because the People of the Book were the root and foundation in these matters, and the polytheists followed them; or because the People of the Book deserved more blame, as they had many scholars and scientists and were thus at a higher level than the polytheists. Therefore, their opposition was uglier, more distasteful, and deserving of great reproach.

Then He reproaches the "People of the Book" and, following them, the "polytheists," saying: Why did they differ in this new religion, some becoming believers and some disbelievers, "while they were not commanded in this religion except to worship Allah, making their devotion sincere to Him, turning away from all polytheism and inclining toward monotheism, establishing prayer, and giving zakat" (وَ ما أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ حُنَفاءَ وَ يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ يُؤْتُوا الزَّكاةَ) [1]. Then He adds: "And that is the upright and lasting religion" (وَ ذلِكَ دِينُ الْقَيِّمَةِ).

Regarding what is meant by "وَ ما أُمِرُوا" (and they were not commanded) here, a group has said it means that in the People of the Book's own religion, the issues of monotheism, prayer, and zakat existed and these are constant matters, yet they did not remain faithful even to these commands.

Another view is that in the religion of Islam, no command has come except for pure monotheism, prayer, zakat, and the like, and these are well-known matters; why then do they refuse to accept it and differ in its acceptance?

The second meaning seems closer, because following the previous verse which spoke of their disagreement in accepting the new religion, it is appropriate that "amiru" refers to the new religion.

Furthermore, the first meaning applies only to the People of the Book and does not include the polytheists, whereas the second meaning includes everyone.

The meaning of "Din" (religion) which they must make sincere for Allah is, according to some commentators, "worship" (ibadah), and the phrase "إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ‌" mentioned before it also supports this meaning. However, it is possible that it refers to the entirety of the religion and Sharia; that is, they were commanded to worship Allah and make their religion and creed sincere in all aspects. This meaning is more consistent with the breadth of the concept of "Din," and the subsequent phrase "وَ ذلِكَ دِينُ الْقَيِّمَةِ," which presents religion in a broad sense, supports this.

"Hunafa" is the plural of "Hanif," from the root "hanaf." According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it means inclining from error toward the straight path. The Arabs used to call everyone who performed the Hajj or was circumcised a "Hanif," indicating they were on the religion of Abraham عليه السلام. "Ahnaf" is said of someone whose foot is turned inward.

Altogether, from various lexicons, it is understood that this word originally meant deviation or crookedness; however, in the Quran and Islamic reports, it is used to mean deviation from polytheism toward monotheism and guidance.

The choice of this expression may originally be because in polytheistic societies, anyone who abandoned their creed and stepped toward monotheism was considered a "Hanif" (deviant), and gradually this term became recognized as a common expression for seekers of the path of monotheism, whose true concept was deviation from "misguidance" to "guidance." Its necessity is pure monotheism, complete moderation, and avoidance of any excess or negligence; however, one must not forget that these are secondary meanings of this word.

The phrase "وَ ذلِكَ دِينُ الْقَيِّمَةِ" [1] indicates that these principles—namely pure monotheism, prayer (attention to the Creator), and zakat (attention to the creation)—are among the firm and stable principles of all religions; rather, it can be said they lie within the very nature (fitrah) of man.

For on one hand, man's destiny is tied to the issue of monotheism, and on the other hand, his nature calls him to thank the Benefactor and to know and recognize Him. On a third hand, man's social spirit and civility call him toward helping the deprived.

Therefore, the roots of these commands, in a general sense, are situated in the depths of all human natures; hence they are placed at the core of the teachings of all previous prophets and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

[1]: The phrase "wa ma amiru" may be a "circumstantial clause" (jumla haliyyah) or an "introductory clause" (isti'nafiyyah), and the "lam" in "li-ya'budu" is the "lam of purpose" (lam al-gharadh). What is meant here is the goal and result that returns to the servants, not a goal that returns to Allah, as some commentators imagined and consequently denied the "lam of purpose." Fundamentally, all of Allah's actions are motivated by purposes, but purposes that return to the servants. Some have considered the "lam" here to mean "an," as in "yuridu Allahu li-yubayyina lakum" (Nisa: 26).

[1]: It should be noted that "Din al-Qayyimah" is in the form of an annexation (idhafah), not a description. Its meaning is that this is the religion that came in the previous "qayyimah" (upright and precious) books, or it is the religion in which the upright and precious laws of Islam are reflected. Therefore, the femininity of "qayyimah" is because it is a description of "kutub" (books) or "millah" (creed) and Sharia (reflect on this).

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمْ شَرُّ ٱلْبَرِيَّةِ (٦)
Indeed the faithless from among the People of the Book and the polytheists will be in the fire of hell, to remain in it [forever]. It is they who are the worst of creatures. (6)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ ٱلْبَرِيَّةِ (٧)
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—it is they who are the best of creatures. (7)
جَزَآؤُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ رَّضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِىَ رَبَّهُۥ (٨)
Their reward, near their Lord, is the Gardens of Eden, with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. Allah is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him. That is for those who fear their Lord. (8)

Commentary

The Best and Worst of Creatures!

In the preceding verses, it was mentioned that the disbelievers among the People of the Book and the polytheists were waiting for a clear proof to come to them from Allah; however, after the "Clear Proof" (Bayyinah) came, they became divided and scattered, each following a different path.

The verses under discussion point to two groups—the "disbelievers" and the "believers"—in the face of this divine invitation, and the ultimate end of each.

First, He says: "Indeed, those who disbelieve from among the People of the Book and the polytheists will be in the fire of Hell, abiding eternally therein. Those are the worst of creatures!" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ وَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ فِي نارِ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها أُولئِكَ هُمْ شَرُّ الْبَرِيَّةِ).

The expression "disbelieved" (كَفَرُوا) refers to their disbelief in the new religion of Islam; otherwise, their prior disbelief and polytheism were nothing new.

The phrase "Those are the worst of creatures" (أُولئِكَ هُمْ شَرُّ الْبَرِيَّةِ) is a jolting expression indicating that among all living and non-living beings, there is no creature more rejected than those who, after the truth has become clear and the proof completed, abandon the right path and step into deviation. This is, in fact, similar to what appears in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 22: "Indeed, the worst of living creatures in the sight of Allah are the deaf and dumb who do not use their intellect" (إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ الصُّمُّ الْبُكْمُ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْقِلُونَ).

Or what is mentioned in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 179, which, after mentioning a group of the inhabitants of Hell with these same attributes, states: "Those are like livestock; rather, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless" (أُولئِكَ كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْغافِلُونَ).

The verse under discussion goes even further than these, as it introduces them as the "worst of creatures," which in reality serves as an explanation for the reason they abide eternally in the fire of Hell.

Why should they not be the worst of creatures when all the doors of prosperity were opened to them, yet they consciously rose in opposition out of pride, arrogance, enmity, and obstinacy?

Placing the "People of the Book" before the "polytheists" in this verse may be because they possessed Divine Scriptures and scholars, and the signs of the Prophet of Islam were explicitly mentioned in their books; therefore, their opposition was uglier and worse.

In the next verse, He refers to the second group, who are their polar opposites and occupy the ascending arc, saying: "Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds—those are the best of creatures" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ أُولئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّةِ).

Then, He describes their reward in a few short phrases: "Their reward with their Lord is gardens of perpetual residence beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever" (جَزاؤُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً).

"Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him" (رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَ رَضُوا عَنْهُ).

"That is for him who has feared his Lord" (ذلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِيَ رَبَّهُ).

It is noteworthy that regarding the believers, the performance of "righteous deeds" is also mentioned, which is in fact the fruit of the tree of faith. This indicates that the mere claim of faith is not sufficient; rather, a person's actions must bear witness to his faith. However, disbelief alone—even if not accompanied by unrighteous deeds—is a cause for downfall and misery; furthermore, disbelief is usually the origin of various sins, crimes, and wrongdoings.

The expression "those are the best of creatures" (أُولئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّةِ) clearly shows that believers who perform righteous deeds are even superior to and higher than the angels, as the verse is absolute and contains no exceptions. Other verses of the Quran also testify to this meaning, such as the verses regarding the angels prostrating to Adam, and the verse: "And We have certainly honored the children of Adam" (وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنا بَنِي آدَمَ - Isra: 70).

In any case, in this verse, He first speaks of their material and physical reward, which is the bountiful gardens of Paradise, and then of their spiritual and psychological reward, which is that Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Allah.

They are pleased with Allah because He has given them whatever they desired, and Allah is pleased with them because they performed whatever He desired; and if there was a slip, He overlooked it by His grace. What pleasure could be greater or higher than for one to feel that he has been accepted and favored by his Beloved and Deity, and has attained His presence?

Everyone has a desire and a goal from You, Our purpose in this world and the next is to meet You!

Yes, the paradise of man's body is the eternal gardens of that world, but the paradise of his soul is the pleasure of Allah and the meeting with the Beloved.

The phrase "That is for him who has feared his Lord" (ذلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِيَ رَبَّهُ) shows that all these blessings stem from "fear and awe of Allah," because this very fear is the motive for moving toward all forms of obedience, piety, and righteous deeds.

Some commentators, by combining this verse with verse 28 of Surah Fatir—"Only those fear Allah, from among His servants, who have knowledge" (إِنَّما يَخْشَى اللَّهَ مِنْ عِبادِهِ الْعُلَماءُ)—have concluded that Paradise is, in fact, the undeniable right of the scholars and the enlightened.

Of course, considering that awe (khashyah) has levels and stages, and knowledge and awareness also possess a hierarchy, the meaning of this statement becomes clear.

Additionally, some believe that the station of khashyah is higher than the station of khawf (fear), because khawf refers to any kind of fear, whereas khashyah is a fear accompanied by glorification and respect.

Key Points

1. Ali (عليه السلام) and His Shi'a are the "Best of Creatures"

In numerous narrations transmitted through Sunni paths and their famous sources, as well as in famous Shi'a sources, the verse "those are the best of creatures" (أُولئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّةِ) has been interpreted as referring to Ali (عليه السلام) and his followers.

"Hakim Haskani Neyshaburi," a famous Sunni scholar of the 5th century AH, narrates these traditions in his well-known book Shawahid al-Tanzil with various chains of transmission, numbering more than twenty narrations. As an example, we consider a few:

  1. Ibn Abbas says: When the verse "Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds—those are the best of creatures" was revealed, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to Ali (عليه السلام): "It is you and your Shi'a; you and your Shi'a will come on the Day of Resurrection well-pleased and well-pleasing, and your enemy will come angry and constrained." (In some versions of the hadith, the word muqmaheen appears, which means having the head held up by shackles and chains). [^1]

  2. In another hadith from Abu Barzah, it is stated that when the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) recited this verse, he said: "They are you and your Shi'a, O Ali! And the meeting place between me and you is the Basin (al-Hawd)." [^2]

  3. In another hadith from Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari, it is stated: We were sitting with the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) by the House of Allah (the Kaaba) when Ali (عليه السلام) came toward us. When the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) eyes fell upon him, he said: "My brother has come to you." Then he turned toward the Kaaba: "And he said: By the Lord of this structure! Indeed, this man and his Shi'a are the successful ones on the Day of Resurrection." Then he turned toward us and added: "By Allah, he was the first of you to believe in Allah, the most steadfast of you in Allah's command, the most faithful of you to Allah's covenant, the most precise of you in judging by Allah's law, the most equitable of you in distribution, the most just of you toward the subjects, and the greatest of you in merit before Allah." Jabir said: Then Allah revealed: "Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds—those are the best of creatures." Thereafter, whenever Ali (عليه السلام) approached, the companions of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) would say: "The best of creatures after the Messenger of Allah has come!" [^1]

The revelation of this verse by the Kaaba does not contradict the Surah being Medinan, as it may be a case of repeated revelation or an application (tatbiq). Furthermore, it is not unlikely that the revelation of these verses occurred during trips the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) made from Medina to Mecca, especially since the narrator is Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari, who joined the Prophet in Medina; labeling such verses as Medinan is not far-fetched.

Some of these hadiths were cited by Ibn Hajar in the book al-Sawa'iq, and some by Muhammad Shiblanji in Nur al-Absar. [^2] Jalaluddin al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur also narrated a major part of the last tradition from Ibn Asakir from Jabir ibn Abdullah. [^3]

  1. In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that when the verse "Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds—those are the best of creatures" was revealed, the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to Ali: "It is you and your Shi'a on the Day of Resurrection, well-pleased and well-pleasing." [^1]

  2. The same author, in another hadith from Ibn Mardawayh, narrates from Ali (عليه السلام) that the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to me: "Have you not heard the word of Allah: 'Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds—those are the best of creatures'? It is you and your Shi'a, and my meeting place with you is the Basin (al-Hawd); when the nations come for the reckoning, you will be called with radiant faces and limbs." [^2]

Many other Sunni scholars have also narrated this same theme in their books, including Khatib al-Khwarizmi in al-Manaqib, Abu Nu'aym al-Isfahani in Kifayat al-Khisam, Allamah Tabari in his famous Tafsir, Ibn Sabbagh al-Maliki in al-Fusul al-Muhimmah, Allamah Shawkani in Fath al-Qadir, Sheikh Sulayman Qunduzi in Yanabi' al-Mawaddah, Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani under the verses in question, and several others.

In short, the aforementioned hadith is among the very well-known and famous traditions accepted by the majority of Islamic scholars, and this is a great and unique virtue for Ali (عليه السلام) and his followers.

Furthermore, from these narrations, the reality clearly emerges that the term "Shi'a" was disseminated among Muslims by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself during his own era, referring to the specific followers of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام). Those who imagine that the term "Shi'a" is an expression that came into existence centuries later are gravely mistaken.

2. The Necessity of Sincerity of Intention in Worship

Some scholars of the principles of jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh) have argued from the verse "And they were not commanded except to worship Allah, [being] sincere to Him in religion" for the necessity of the "intention of proximity" (qasdu al-qurbah) in acts of worship, and that the default in commands is that they are devotional (ta'abbudi) rather than merely procedural (tawassuli). This depends on "religion" (deen) here meaning "worship," so it may serve as proof for the necessity of sincerity in worship, and on treating the "command" in this verse as absolute so that its meaning is the necessity of the intention of proximity in all commands (except cases excluded by proof). However, the meaning of the verse apparently is neither of these; rather, the objective is to establish monotheism (Tawhid) in opposition to polytheism (Shirk)—meaning they were not invited except to monotheism—and as such, it does not relate to secondary legal rulings.

3. The Extraordinary Ascending and Descending Arcs of Man

From the verses of this Surah, it is well understood that no creature in the universe has a distance between its ascending and descending arcs as vast as man. If he possesses faith and righteous deeds (note that "done righteous deeds" includes all righteous acts, not just some), he is the best of Allah's creation. But if he follows the path of disbelief, misguidance, obstinacy, and enmity, he falls so far that he becomes the worst of Allah's creation! This massive gap between the "ascending arc" and the "descending arc" of man, though a sensitive and dangerous matter, indicates the greatness of the human species' station and its capacity for perfection. It is natural that alongside such extraordinary capacity and talent, the possibility of extraordinary decline and fall also exists.

O Allah! We seek help from Your grace to reach the lofty station of the "Best of Creatures." O Lord! Place us among the Shi'a and followers of that great man who is most worthy of this title. O God! Grant us such sincerity that we worship none but You and love none but You.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Bayyinah.

[^1]: Shawahid al-Tanzil, Vol. 2, p. 357, Hadith 1126. [^2]: Ibid, p. 359, Hadith 1130. [^3]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 379. [^4]: Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah, p. 96; Nur al-Absar, pp. 70 and 101.
